back_translation: Paul traveled and arrived at the city of Derbe and the city of Lystra, and behold, there was a certain disciple there named Timothy, the son of a certain Jewish woman who was a believer; the father was a Greek.
Timothy had a good reputation among the brothers in the city of Lystra and the city of Iconium.
Paul wanted Timothy to travel together with him, so he had him circumcised on account of the Jews who were in that area, because everyone knew well that his father was a Greek.

While they traveled through the various cities, they delivered to the believers in those cities the regulations which the apostles and church elders in the city of Jerusalem had decided, for them to observe.
Therefore the churches grew strong in the faith, and increased in number every day.
They traveled through the province of Phrygia and the province of Galatia, the Holy Spirit having forbidden them not to speak the word in the province of Asia.
When they came to the border of the province of Mysia, they tried to enter the province of Bithynia, but the Spirit of Jesus did not permit them to go.
So they passed through the province of Mysia and went down to the city of Troas.
In that night Paul saw a vision — there was a certain Macedonian man standing and pleading with him, saying, "Please cross over to the province of Macedonia and help us."
When Paul had seen that vision, immediately we sought to travel to the province of Macedonia, concluding that God had called us to proclaim the good news to them.
So we set sail from the city of Troas, sailed straight to the island of Samothrace, and the next day arrived at the city of Neapolis.
From there we traveled on to the city of Philippi, which is an important city in the district of the province of Macedonia, a Roman colony. We stayed in this city for many days.
On the Sabbath day we went outside the city gate by the river, which we understood to be a place of prayer; so we sat down and conversed with the women who were gathered there.

There was a certain woman named Lydia, a person of the city of Thyatira, a merchant of purple cloth, a worshiper of God, who was listening; the Lord opened her heart to attend to the words that Paul spoke.

When she and the people of her household had received baptism, she pleaded with us, saying, "If you all consider me truly to be one having faith in the Lord, please come and stay in my house." And she entreated us so earnestly that we yielded.

One day while we were going to the place of prayer, a certain slave girl who had a spirit [Python — the snake-deity of prophecy of the ancient Greeks] met us; she had brought a great profit to her masters by fortune-telling.
She walked following Paul and us, shouting, "These men are servants of the Most High God, who are proclaiming the way of salvation to you all!"

She did this for many days. Eventually Paul became greatly annoyed, so he turned and commanded that spirit, "In the name of Jesus Christ, we command you to come out of her." And the spirit came out at once.
When her masters saw that their opportunity for profit had ceased, they seized Paul and Silas and dragged them to the city-center plaza to meet the ruling officials.
They brought Paul and Silas to the city magistrates and said, "These men are causing disturbance in our city — they are Jews,
and are propagating various customs which are unlawful for us, who are Romans, to receive or to practice."
The crowd united in attacking Paul and Silas, so the city magistrates had their clothes torn off and ordered them flogged with rods.
When they had been beaten severely, they seized the two of them and threw them into prison, and ordered the jailer to keep guard over them strictly.
When the jailer received such an order, he confined the two of them in the inner chamber of the prison and locked their feet in wooden stocks.
About midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing songs of praise to God, and the other prisoners were listening attentively.
Suddenly a great earthquake occurred so that the foundations of the prison shook; immediately every door opened, and the chains of everyone came loose.
When the jailer woke up and saw the prison doors open, he drew his sword to kill himself, because he thought that the prisoners had all escaped.
But Paul shouted with a loud voice, "Do not harm yourself; we are all still here."
The jailer asked for lamps, ran inside, and trembling with fear, fell down before Paul and Silas.
He led the two of them outside and asked, "Sirs, what must I do to be saved?"
The two answered, "Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved — both you and your household."
Then Paul and Silas spoke the word of the Lord to the jailer and to everyone who was in his house.
At that late hour of the night, the jailer took the two of them to wash the wounds from the flogging, and the jailer and everyone in his household received baptism immediately.
Then the jailer brought the two of them up to his house, prepared food for them to eat, and rejoiced together with all his household because they had come to believe in God.
When morning came, the city magistrates sent the rod-bearing officers to inform, "Release those men."
So the jailer reported these words to Paul: "The city magistrates have sent men to say to release you both. Therefore now please go out in peace."

But Paul said to the officers, "They flogged us in public without a judicial procedure, although we are Roman citizens, and threw us into prison; and now they want to release us secretly? No — let them come themselves and lead us out instead."
The rod-bearing officers reported these words to the city magistrates, and the magistrates were alarmed when they heard that Paul and Silas were Roman citizens.
So the magistrates came to ask reconciliation with Paul and Silas, then led the two of them out of the prison, asking them to leave the city.
When they had come out of the prison, Paul and Silas went to the house of Lydia, met the brothers there, encouraged them, and then continued on their journey.
